title: Migrating right lower quadrant pain in a reproductive-age woman

chart.oneLiner: 26F with PCOS, a copper IUD, and a prior treated chlamydial PID presents with ~20 hours of abdominal pain that began around the umbilicus and migrated to the right lower quadrant, with anorexia, nausea, and low-grade fever; her last period was uncertain (~7 weeks ago) and her cycles are irregular.

opening: It started up around my belly button yesterday morning, and now it's all moved down to my lower right side and it really hurts.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 26-year-old woman, lying still on the stretcher (movement and the car ride worsened the pain). Anxious but cooperative; worried because she is not sure when her last period was.

HPI: Pain began ~20h ago, dull and periumbilical, then over several hours MIGRATED to the right lower quadrant where it is now sharp and constant. Anorexia (skipped dinner and breakfast — nothing sounds good), nausea, two episodes of vomiting. Subjective low-grade fevers/chills. Worse with movement, coughing, and going over bumps in the car. NO diarrhea. NO dysuria, frequency, or flank pain. NO vaginal bleeding and NO abnormal discharge today. NO sudden severe pelvic pain that came and went (no intermittent twisting/colicky pelvic pain). The pain did NOT start in the pelvis or one ovary — it started up high near the navel and walked down.
GYN/REPRODUCTIVE (reveal these only when asked the relevant question): Last menstrual period UNCERTAIN — she thinks about 7 weeks ago, but her periods are irregular so she is not sure. Cycles irregular and unpredictable (known PCOS). Has a copper (non-hormonal) IUD placed ~2 years ago, never checked the strings. Sexually active with one partner, inconsistent barrier use. Could she be pregnant? — she assumed not because of the IUD, but admits she can't rule it out. Treated for chlamydia with pelvic infection (PID) about 3 years ago with antibiotics; no surgeries.
PMH: PCOS; prior chlamydial pelvic inflammatory disease (treated). No diabetes. MEDS: metformin for PCOS; occasional ibuprofen; no hormonal contraception (copper IUD only); no anticoagulants. ALLERGIES: none. SOCIAL: works as a barista, social alcohol on weekends, no tobacco, no drugs. FAMILY: noncontributory. ROS: as above; no urinary symptoms, no vaginal bleeding/discharge today, no shoulder-tip pain, no lightheadedness/syncope.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: uncomfortable, lying still, mild distress.
- Vitals: BP 118/72, HR 102, RR 18, 38.0°C, SpO2 99% RA.
- Abdomen: maximal tenderness at McBurney's point (RLQ) with voluntary guarding and rebound tenderness; positive Rovsing sign; positive psoas sign. Mild suprapubic tenderness. No CVA tenderness. No distension.
- Pelvic/adnexal (if the student elicits the need for a bimanual/pelvic exam in history): right adnexal/lower-quadrant tenderness; NO adnexal mass, NO cervical motion tenderness, NO vaginal bleeding, NO purulent cervical discharge. (Findings argue against torsion mass and against active PID/abscess.)
- Bowel sounds present. Cardiac/lungs: normal.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the migration pattern unless asked how/where the pain started and moved.
- Do NOT volunteer the LMP, IUD, possible-pregnancy concern, or prior PID unless asked about periods, contraception, pregnancy, or gynecologic/sexual history.
- The peritoneal and pelvic signs are revealed only if the relevant exam is performed/raised.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.
- Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.CBC: WBC 15.1 with left shift (neutrophil predominance, bands present), Hgb 11.5, Plt 281.
labs.BMP: Within normal limits; normal renal function and electrolytes.
labs.UA: A few WBCs, no nitrites, no significant hematuria, no bacteria; urine hCG not relied upon — serum hCG sent.
labs.betaHCG: Serum beta-hCG POSITIVE but low at 350 mIU/mL — confirms pregnancy; the level alone cannot localize it, so imaging must locate the pregnancy.
labs.pelvic_ultrasound: Transvaginal/pelvic ultrasound (chosen over CT given the positive pregnancy test): a single small INTRAUTERINE gestational sac confirming an early intrauterine pregnancy (lowers ectopic); NORMAL bilateral ovarian arterial AND venous flow with normal-volume ovaries and no adnexal mass (lowers torsion); no significant free fluid. In the right lower quadrant there is a dilated, non-compressible, blind-ending tubular structure measuring ~9 mm with surrounding hyperemia, consistent with acute appendicitis.

ekg.findings: Sinus tachycardia at ~102 bpm; normal axis and intervals; no acute ischemic changes.

ekg.clinicalVignette: A young adult with abdominal pain, dehydration, and low-grade fever has a fast but regular pulse on examination.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Clear lung fields; no free air under the diaphragm; normal cardiomediastinal silhouette.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Clear lung fields; no free air under the diaphragm; normal cardiomediastinal silhouette. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph with clear lungs and no subdiaphragmatic free air
searchTerms: no free air under diaphragm chest radiograph
cxr.clinicalVignette: A young adult with acute abdominal pain has a screening upright chest film to exclude free air.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical in this reproductive-age woman, list your differential diagnosis (note your leading diagnosis). Explicitly name three must-not-miss diagnoses for right-lower-quadrant pain in a reproductive-age woman.
steps.rubric: Acute appendicitis (leading,5). Ectopic pregnancy — must-not-miss given uncertain LMP and IUD (CANT-MISS,4). Ovarian torsion — must-not-miss (CANT-MISS,4). Ruptured hemorrhagic ovarian cyst — must-not-miss (CANT-MISS,2). Pelvic inflammatory disease / tubo-ovarian abscess given prior chlamydial PID (CANT-MISS,2). UTI / pyelonephritis (core,1). Nephrolithiasis / ureteric colic (core,1). Names leading dx w/ justification — ~20h migration, anorexia, peritoneal signs (core,4). Explicitly states a pregnancy test is mandatory before committing in any reproductive-age woman with RLQ pain (core,3). PENALTY: anchors on appendicitis without considering pregnancy/gynecologic emergencies (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Acute appendicitis (classic ~20h migratory periumbilical-to-RLQ pain with anorexia and peritoneal signs). Three (or more) must-not-miss diagnoses for RLQ pain in a reproductive-age woman: Ectopic pregnancy, Ovarian torsion, and Ruptured hemorrhagic ovarian cyst — plus Pelvic inflammatory disease / tubo-ovarian abscess given her prior chlamydial PID. The uncertain LMP and copper IUD do NOT exclude pregnancy, so a serum beta-hCG is mandatory before committing. Also reasonable: UTI/pyelonephritis and nephrolithiasis.

coreActions.item: Named leading dx w/ justification — migration, anorexia, peritoneal signs
States a pregnancy test is mandatory in any reproductive-age woman with RLQ pain

penalties.item: Anchors on appendicitis without considering pregnancy/gynecologic emergencies

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial diagnostic workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: Serum beta-hCG FIRST in any reproductive-age woman with abdominal pain (CANT-MISS,4). Pelvic/transvaginal ULTRASOUND as imaging of choice — avoid CT radiation given possible/confirmed pregnancy (CANT-MISS,4). CBC (3). BMP (1). Urinalysis to exclude urinary cause (1). Lipase (1). Pelvic exam +/- cervical NAAT/wet prep given prior PID (bonus,1). PENALTY: ordering CT abdomen/pelvis before a pregnancy test (-2); shotgun panel w/ no reasoning (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: serum beta-hCG FIRST; CBC; BMP; Urinalysis; Lipase; pelvic/transvaginal ultrasound as the imaging of choice (defer/avoid CT given a possible pregnancy and the radiation to ovaries/fetus); EKG; CXR. Consider a pelvic exam with cervical NAAT given her prior chlamydial PID.

criticalActions.item: Pelvic / transvaginal ultrasound (avoid CT given possible pregnancy)

bonusActions.item: Pelvic exam +/- cervical NAAT given prior PID

penalties.item: Ordering CT abdomen/pelvis before a pregnancy test

requiredTests: Pelvic / transvaginal ultrasound (avoid CT given possible pregnancy)
contraindicatedTests: Ordering CT abdomen/pelvis before a pregnancy test

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus tachycardia (~102 bpm) with normal axis and intervals and no acute ischemic changes; the tachycardia is explained by pain, fever, and dehydration. CREDIT: identifies sinus tachycardia (7); recognizes normal intervals/axis with no acute ischemia (5); systematic read (3). PENALTY: over-reading a benign sinus tachycardia as a primary cardiac event (-3).
criticalFindings: Sinus tachycardia with no acute ischemic changes.
commonMistakes: calling sinus tachycardia a primary arrhythmia
over-reading benign T-wave changes as ischemia
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus tachycardia at ~102 bpm with normal axis and intervals and no acute ST/T changes — consistent with pain, fever, and volume depletion rather than a primary cardiac process.

criticalActions: Sinus tachycardia with no acute ischemic changes.

penalties.label: over-reads a benign sinus tachycardia as a primary cardiac event

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = clear lungs, NO free air under the diaphragm, normal cardiomediastinal silhouette. CREDIT: recognizes a normal film (5); specifically states there is no subdiaphragmatic free air to suggest a perforated viscus (5). PENALTY: missing the absence of free air as a deliberate pertinent negative, or inventing pathology (-3).
keyFindings: no free air under the diaphragm
criticalFindings: No subdiaphragmatic free air; otherwise normal chest film.
commonMistakes: failing to comment on the absence of free air
inventing an infiltrate on a clear film
steps.idealAnswer: Clear lung fields, normal cardiomediastinal silhouette, and — importantly — no free air under the diaphragm to suggest a perforated viscus.

criticalActions: No subdiaphragmatic free air; otherwise normal chest film.
coreActions: no free air under the diaphragm

penalties.label: calls the study abnormal / invents pathology

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Given the beta-hCG and ultrasound findings, what is your diagnosis and reasoning? Address how the pregnancy reframes your imaging and which gynecologic emergencies you have now excluded.
steps.rubric: Acute appendicitis confirmed on ultrasound (dilated, non-compressible, blind-ending tubular RLQ structure ~9 mm with hyperemia), now occurring in a confirmed early INTRAUTERINE pregnancy (6). Explains the positive beta-hCG drove ultrasound-over-CT and mandates OB involvement; names the discriminators that excluded the must-not-miss alternatives — intrauterine sac lowers ectopic, normal bilateral ovarian arterial+venous flow lowers torsion, no adnexal mass/free fluid argues against ruptured cyst, no cervical motion tenderness/discharge argues against active PID (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Acute appendicitis — ultrasound shows a dilated, non-compressible, blind-ending tubular structure (~9 mm) with hyperemia in the RLQ, supported by the leukocytosis with left shift (WBC 15.1) and the classic ~20h migratory pain with anorexia and peritoneal signs. The picture is reframed by a POSITIVE beta-hCG (350 mIU/mL): she has a confirmed early intrauterine pregnancy on ultrasound. Commit to appendicitis. The positive pregnancy test is exactly why ultrasound was chosen over CT (avoid fetal/ovarian radiation) and why OB must be involved. Discriminators that excluded the gynecologic must-not-misses: the intrauterine gestational sac makes ectopic very unlikely; normal bilateral ovarian arterial AND venous flow with normal ovaries and no mass excludes torsion; no adnexal mass or significant free fluid argues against a ruptured hemorrhagic cyst; and no cervical motion tenderness or purulent discharge argues against active PID/tubo-ovarian abscess.

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan, including disposition and counseling for this pregnant patient?
steps.rubric: Per MGH abdominal pain / surgical abdomen in pregnancy: NPO + IV fluids (CANT-MISS,4); urgent general surgery consult for appendectomy WITH OB/obstetrics co-management (CANT-MISS,6); pregnancy-appropriate antibiotics (2); pregnancy-safe analgesia + antiemetics (3); imaging-choice rationale — ultrasound over CT to avoid fetal/ovarian radiation (3); check Rh status and give RhoGAM if Rh-negative (2); serial abdominal exams (2); safe-discharge/perioperative counseling for the pregnancy + close OB follow-up (3). PENALTY: discharging a surgical abdomen (-4); managing without OB involvement / ignoring the pregnancy (-3); ordering CT now that an IUP is confirmed (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: NPO, IV fluids. URGENT general surgery consultation for appendectomy WITH OB/obstetrics co-management given the confirmed early intrauterine pregnancy. Pregnancy-appropriate antibiotics (e.g., a beta-lactam regimen safe in pregnancy). Pregnancy-safe analgesia and antiemetics. Continue ultrasound-based imaging and avoid CT radiation now that an IUP is confirmed. Check Rh status and give RhoGAM if Rh-negative. Serial abdominal exams. Counsel the patient on the operative plan, anesthetic/fetal safety, and the importance of close OB follow-up for the pregnancy after appendectomy.

criticalActions.item: Urgent surgery consult for appendectomy with OB/obstetrics co-management

coreActions.item: Ultrasound over CT to avoid fetal/ovarian radiation
Check Rh status and give RhoGAM if Rh-negative
Safe-discharge/perioperative counseling for the pregnancy and close OB follow-up

penalties.item: Managing without OB involvement / ignoring the pregnancy

requiredActions: Urgent surgery consult for appendectomy with OB/obstetrics co-management
optionalActions: Ultrasound over CT to avoid fetal/ovarian radiation
Check Rh status and give RhoGAM if Rh-negative
Safe-discharge/perioperative counseling for the pregnancy and close OB follow-up
unsafeActions: Managing without OB involvement / ignoring the pregnancy

standardizedPatient.communicationStyle: answers questions asked without volunteering diagnostic clues
standardizedPatient.affect: 26-year-old woman, lying still (movement worsens pain), anxious about a possible pregnancy. Cooperative.
disclosureRules: Do not reveal sexual history, pregnancy concern, contraception, or prior STI unless directly asked
Do not mention or hint at the diagnosis
Do not volunteer hidden exam or diagnostic findings
Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing

Mild suprapubic and right lower-quadrant tenderness; no rigidity. (Raises the need to consider a pelvic/adnexal source.)
No costovertebral angle tenderness (argues against pyelonephritis/renal colic).
Abdomen not distended; no surgical scars; no visible masses.

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 26F with PCOS, a copper IUD, and a prior treated chlamydial PID presents with ~20 hours of abdominal pain that began around the umbilicus and migrated to the right lower quadrant, with anorexia, nausea, and low-grade fever, on a background of an uncertain LMP (~7 weeks ago). The diagnosis is Acute appendicitis, confirmed on ultrasound, but reframed by an incidental positive beta-hCG showing an early intrauterine pregnancy. It is reached by eliciting the focused history (including the reproductive/sexual history), performing the targeted exam, sending a mandatory pregnancy test, choosing ultrasound over CT, and excluding the gynecologic must-not-miss alternatives (ectopic, ovarian torsion, ruptured cyst, PID).
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Abdomen: maximal tenderness at McBurney's point (RLQ) with voluntary guarding and rebound tenderness; positive Rovsing sign; positive psoas sign. Mild suprapubic tenderness. No distension.

Abdomen: maximal tenderness at McBurney's point (RLQ) with voluntary guarding and rebound tenderness; positive Rovsing sign; positive psoas sign. Mild suprapubic tenderness. No distension.
Mild suprapubic and right lower-quadrant tenderness; no rigidity. (Raises the need to consider a pelvic/adnexal source.)
No costovertebral angle tenderness (argues against pyelonephritis/renal colic).
Abdomen not distended; no surgical scars; no visible masses.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including the reproductive/sexual history (LMP, contraception, prior PID) only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings, and recognize the need for a pelvic/adnexal assessment.
Build a differential led by Acute appendicitis while explicitly naming the gynecologic must-not-miss causes (ectopic, ovarian torsion, ruptured cyst, PID).
Send a mandatory serum beta-hCG and choose pelvic ultrasound over CT; interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Acute appendicitis reframed by a confirmed intrauterine pregnancy, and manage with stabilization, appendectomy under OB co-management, RhoGAM if Rh-negative, and disposition.
commonPitfalls: Anchoring on appendicitis without sending a pregnancy test
Ordering CT before/after confirming pregnancy and irradiating an early IUP
managementPearls: Urgent surgery consult for appendectomy with OB/obstetrics co-management
criticalWorkupItems: Pelvic / transvaginal ultrasound (avoid CT given possible pregnancy)
criticalInterpretationItems: Sinus tachycardia with no acute ischemic changes — consistent with pain, fever, and volume depletion.
Normal chest film with no subdiaphragmatic free air to suggest a perforated viscus.
criticalManagementItems: Urgent surgery consult for appendectomy with OB/obstetrics co-management
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus tachycardia with no acute ischemic changes.
No subdiaphragmatic free air; otherwise normal chest film.
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus tachycardia with no acute ischemic changes.
No subdiaphragmatic free air; otherwise normal chest film.
imageInterpretationPearls: A fast but regular rhythm in a febrile, dehydrated, painful patient is sinus tachycardia — explain it, do not over-work it up.
On an abdominal-pain chest film, the deliberate pertinent negative is the absence of free air under the diaphragm.
Read every study systematically and state pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Asks permission before sensitive sexual/reproductive questions
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
